speaker: interviewer
text: 1994, Los Angeles, Dana Schwartz, tape two. Mary--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just wait till-- I'm  still on a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So just begin telling the story, when-- when I go like this, about what happened when you came back from the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you comfortable? What happened in September '30-- 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we were hiding out for a while. And then we-- one day, during the day, we were outside. We never expected anything during the day. We were always afraid something will happen during the night. And sure enough, the man that I spoke to you about before, the German, came with two Germans in uniform. And the first thing he said to my dad was, I came to you with the Hitler [GERMAN]. And my father didn't blink an eye. And he said to them, gentlemen, sit down. He took a bottle of whisky. He says, let's have a drink, and let's discuss it as civilized people. He said, I'm sure you told these people what happened. And I would like to give my side of the story. And they said, fine. They had a drink. And they said, OK, tell us. And my father proceeded to explain to them what happened and how it happened. And lo and behold, they believed my father. And they said, look, shake hands and make up. And we were shocked. And my father then turned around to us, after they left, and he said, you see, I told you. There is no people like the German people. His experience went to the First World War, when he was with the Russian Army. And he was taken prisoner of war by the Germans. So he always used to tell us that he was better off in the German prison than in the Russian Army, that they gave him more respect, and they were much nicer than the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think this was what he always carried with him, that the Germans were the intelligentsia, and they were the, you know, people of-- of letters. And-- and he just loved them. He-- he always attached himself to German people.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that incident, my mother got scared. And she said, look, you don't know. He-- he shook hands with you, because they made him. But he might try to do something. Let's go and get lost. Let's find another place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this point, we had entered-- went to Russia, supposedly. She never did get there. But-- so when she left, she gave my dad the keys to her apartment. So we went over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beside us, there was one other Jewish family in the building. They lived downstairs. We lived on the first floor. And they had a girl my age, a little girl. And I spent most of the time playing with her. It was getting already to a time where the Germans insisted that the Jews go into the ghetto. And my father did not want to go into the ghetto. He just didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there. But at this point, we had to start wearing yellow armbands. And then it turned into yellow stars. And we had no food. So my sister decided, one day, that she's gonna go. She took off the marks of a Jew. And she went trying to do a little shopping. And she was gone. And about an hour later, the janitor of the building came, and he said to my parents. He said, you know, your daughter was arrested. And my father says, how do you know? He says, well, she's coming with two SS men. And we couldn't understand. If she was arrested, why would she be coming home? But then my mother said to my father, you see, I told you. We should have gone into the ghetto. And they got very scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this point, there was a knock on the door. And my mother opened the door. And it was like unbelievable. Because those two SS men, in uniforms, grab my mother and started hugging her and kissing her. And my sister came with two big bundles of food. And these two fellas happened to have been friends of my oldest brother. And they-- they saw her. She was standing in line. And they took her immediately in. And they said-- they-- they bought all this stuff for her. And they said, let's go to your parents. We want to see your family. And that's how she brought them home. But things were getting kind of rough. We heard, at night, screaming. We heard beatings. We heard crying, and-- because our windows were out-- overlooking the street. Of course, we had shades on them. We even afraid to look. But it was getting kind of hairy. And one night, we heard terrible screams. And my father went down. And he came and told my brother to go down with him, because the Germans had just beaten a Jewish man almost to death, and that he's laying on the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother and my dad brought this man up to our place. And I remember, they put him on the floor in the kitchen. And that's where he died. And right after that, the following day, my father said, OK, now we gonna go. So we packed up whatever belongings we had and whatever belongings my aunt left. I don't know exactly how my father arranged it, but we did get a beautiful apartment in the ghetto. Which was-- it-- it just was an impossibility at this point, because all the people from the city were moving into the ghetto. And we happened to have a beautiful apartment. Where did he get the money for this apartment? He hadn't been working, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, he hadn't been-- well, we did make a lot of money at the beginning. When the Germans came in, my father went to see the occupying forces. And he explained to him how important it is for young people, who come from the front, to have a place to go and dance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did get a special permit. But there was a-- a attached clause to it that we have to put a sign saying it's a Jewish business. And, at the same time, we have to have someone running it who is German. So we had a German cleaning girl. So my father took her, and she got a-- a pass or whatever papers she needed. And supposedly, she was running the business. It was on her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was quite a few problems though, also, with some Nazis, who found out that it was a Jewish business. And they used to come. And they used to start fights. And then a big, big officer came. And he spoke to the German soldiers. And he told them, it's a disgrace that they came to a Jewish business. And they got scared, and-- and they stopped coming. And that's when-- He did also have, one night, a few words with my father, where he proceeded to beating him. And he beat him pretty badly. And at this point, my father closed the business. But coming back to the time we came into the ghetto, we had this very lovely apartment. And we met the people that lived above us and below us. And it was still a time of-- you know, it's a transition, but it's not terrible. And at the beginning, we had some-- quite a few good times with neighbors, with stories, and everybody would tell their story. And it-- it was pretty comfortable and nice. It didn't last too long. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1940. Or beginning of 19-- no, the winter of 1940 or '41, I'm not sure. And we heard that they, the Germans, choose a man to be the head of the ghetto. And then we heard that there's gonna be a police department and a fire department. And-- We-- we didn't pay attention to it, because we still were pretty comfortable. But I think it must've been the winter of 1941 when there was no more shops. There was no more food to be gotten, not even on the black market. There was no more coal. There was nothing. At this point, the-- the winter was pretty heavy. And even if we got some food, we couldn't cook it. There was no fuel. So I remember that as the beginning of terrible, terrible times. Because people started to destroy. First, they were destroying their furniture. And we started to-- I remember, we had a beautiful dining room table, which we chopped up, and then, next winter, chairs. And you know, slowly, the apartment became empty of any furniture. Anything that would burn, we burned. And some people, who had nothing, started to take apart the building. And one day, one step was missing, the front stoop. The next, the door to the-- because we had no bathrooms. It was a common bathroom at-- on the yard. Then, one day, there was no door to the bathroom. And slowly, people just took everything apart to use it as fuel. And it got very cold. And we had nothing. And it got so cold that the walls in the apartment had about half an inch of ice on them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now I understand that from the-- just from the human body, or-- or-- you know, the breath, it just settle on the cold walls, and  it became ice. And this-- this was terrible. I remember being very cold. Then, slowly, the staircase was disappearing. And we afraid that we'll be prisoners in the apartment. We won't be able to come down. So my father started looking for another place. And he found a little house around the corner that the people left. We were told they went to Warsaw. So we moved down-- down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to get my thoughts together. What happened? Oh, yeah, we had no food. So there was no way to-- to buy or-- there's no way you could get any food. So one day, my oldest brother said to my sister, you know, Irene, you're gonna have to go out of the ghetto and get some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, fine. So she started-- at-- at that time, the Germans made a fence, a wire fence around the ghetto. But there was nobody watching the fence. So you still could do that. You could go through the fence and take off your signs of being a Jew and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she used to go and do some of the shopping. Then she would come back. And she would-- we would sell part of it for other things that-- you know, it was like a trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and it became a big thing. Almost everybody had someone in the family who was smuggling, someone who didn't look Jewish. It didn't take long for the Germans to get wise to it. And once they got wise to it, they started to check the people, who were walking near the fence, on the Polish side, for passports. And if you didn't have a passport, they knew immediately that you were a Jew. And one day, we were told that my sister was arrested. So my father went to the building where the Jewish people, who were in charge-- of the-- the Jewish police, already. And they said, yes, she is in this central place, which was called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means [NON-ENGLISH] was the name of the section where the ghetto was. [NON-ENGLISH] means a market, which was, before the war, but, at this time, it was a place where the Germans used to come in to ghetto through that. They had like a compound there. They-- they stayed there. And all the business was transacted through this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So my father went-- we all went to see where she was and try to bring her home, which we did, because he had a little influence. He knew-- a lot of people knew him. And they released her. But she was so badly beaten, that she had to be brought home on a stretcher. And she proceeded to lay for almost three weeks. She couldn't move. Her back-- this was the first time in my life I saw scars on someone's back. It was just black. It was just raw, bloody. And that's how she ended her career as a smuggler. Well, it didn't take long. We ran out of food again. And at this point, I told my brother that I want to go. And he said, OK. So he told me exactly where to go. Because where my sister used to go was also someone we knew before the war, who was a Volksdeutsche. He happened to have a store not too far away from the ghetto. So my brother said, if you go to Mr. Muller, he will sell you all the things. And at the same time, you won't have to walk far on the Polish side. So you are not taking such a big chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was almost 11. So I went. And I started my career as a smuggler. And I would go out in the morning, cross the fence, go to the Mullers's, do my shopping, and come back. And my brothers would be waiting. And they would help me come through the fence. First, they would take the packages, and then they would hold the barbed wire. Once again, the Germans decided to do something about it. So they put sentries on each, I would say, each block. Each corner, they would put a sentry. And it got pretty rough, because you only could cross when the sentry's back was turned or if he just disappeared for a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you'd be standing there and watching. And you would have a string of people, who would be watching the sentry. And one would tell the other, he's gone, or he's turning his back. And that's how we would run through the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was on the Polish side, I saw my two cousins. And I saw a bunch of Polish kids running after them and yelling, Jew, Jew, Jew. And it didn't take long. The police came. And they arrested my cousins. I was pretty shook up by it. But I had already bought my stuff. And I was on the way back. And I just ignored the whole thing. And as I was walking close to the fence, normally, my brothers would give me a sign when to come through. But they were shaking their heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just kept on going. And I circled the block about two or three times. And finally, at one time, while I was circling, I saw two policemen coming toward me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one was dressed as a Volksdeutsche in a black uniform, and one wore the German uniform. And they approached me. And they asked me for  my passport. They were talking in German. And I was smart enough not to respond, because I figured, if I speak, if I let them know that I understand, they will think-- they will be positive that I'm a Jew. Because most Jewish people understood German, because the Jewish language and the German language is very similar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I made believe that I didn't understand what they wanted. At this point, a Polish woman came over, and she said, ah, ah. She says, she's my neighbor's daughter. My neighbor's sick, so she sent her for food shopping.

speaker: interviewee
text: She tried to help me. So she said, oh, let me take her back to her mother. And I-- I don't know what happened. I just-- I've tried to analyze it a million times. I can't. I just said, no. I'm not Polish. I am a Jew. It just came out of me. I just, I don't know. I-- I was just about to be saved. This woman was gonna take me into the building. But I said, no. I'm not ashamed of it. I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, of course, they arrested me. Now, they took me to a precinct. And I was sitting there. And it started to rain. And no one knew what to do with me in that police precinct. All the Germans were walking around, and they were looking at me. And they were saying, what is she doing here? Well, she's a smuggler. A young one like that? You know, they just couldn't believe it. And finally, at one point, it started to rain. It was coming down very hard. And I heard one of the policemen behind the counter, there, said to the other one, do you have a cigarette? And he said, no. I-- I have no more cigarettes. And he said, oh, man, look at this weather. He says, how am I gonna go to get some cigarettes? So I got up from the bench, and I went over and I said, I have cigarettes. I said, in my package, I have a carton of cigarettes. And he says, you do? You know, I said, yeah. So he took a pack of cigarettes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it so happened that, about 20 minutes later, he was the one that they told, when the big man in charge came in, he says, you take her to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was where my sister was, where they beat her almost to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said to this fellow, to this policeman, you take her there. And he tried to get out of it. He says, it's raining. They-- Let's wait. No, you have to take her. She's a Jewess, and she has to go there. That's the orders. So he started walking with me. And in order to go to this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], we had to walk the same street that I used to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go out from the ghetto from. And there was a sentry there, in a little-- sentry-- little-- what do you call them? It's like a box or a little house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So as we were passing there--

speaker: interviewee
text: A station.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the-- the fellow in the back said to the guy, the policeman that was walking with me, he said, where are you going in this rain? He says, oh, I got a Jewess here, that I have to deliver her to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So they started talking. And-- and he said, do you have a cigarette? He says, yeah, I have a cigarette. He  took out the package he took from me. And they were smoking and talking. And finally, he said to the guy, to the-- to the sentry, he says, man, he said, it's raining so badly. He says, it's such a long way to this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Why can't I just let her go in, here, through the fence? So the other fellow says, well, he said, I'll tell you what, I'll turn my back and let her go. So I I-- I heard it. I couldn't believe it. So he said to me, OK, get going. So  I ran to the fence. But I was so nervous. And the fences were  very tight. And I couldn't pick it up to spread it to go through, because, usually, my brothers used to do that for me. So he came over, and he spread it for me. And he said, my god, how can you be a smuggler if you can't even pick up the fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was laughing. And he let me through. Well, and I came home. It was like an unbelievable miracle happened. Because my whole family, my-- my father found out. Because, all the time, my father didn't know that I was smuggling. Because my father thought that my brother was smuggling. And he would never allow me to do that. But at this point, my father found out, my mother found out. They said, never again. You're not gonna go. But I continued going, because it was a necessity.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was going until, one day, the sentry was shooting at me. And after that, when I made it into the ghetto, my brother decided. And I said, never again. I was so scared. After that, I said, no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it started-- we heard stories that, on this side of the ghetto, five kids were shot here, two kids. All over, they were already shooting to kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you are bringing this stuff, what was in the bags usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, there was bread. There was flour. There was sugar. Sometimes there was butter, cigarettes, just staples. And this is what kept us going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you quit running through the fence--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it. Everything stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened to your food supply?

speaker: interviewee
text: No more. There was no more food supply. And there was no-- the ghetto wasn't organized yet-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --to give us rations. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it-- it-- it was pretty bad. We-- it was starvation. It was actually starvation. So at this point, my dad decided that, since we had a nice piece of land, if we could only get some seeds.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].
